=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - **Yoshitake Takagi Site** ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - **Ōgōri Wakayama Site** ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - **Uki Kumida Site** ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Masuda Site** ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - **Satodahara Site** ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - **Kajikuri Beach Site** ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Nagura Site** ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ōgōri Site** ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - **Bancheon-ri Site** ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ipsil-ri Site** ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - **Yoshitake Takagi Site** ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - **Ōgōri Wakayama Site** ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - **Uki Kumida Site** ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Masuda Site** ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - **Satodahara Site** ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - **Kajikuri Beach Site** ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Nagura Site** ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ōgōri Site** ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - **Bancheon-ri Site** ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ipsil-ri Site** ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Tachū Saimonkyō}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Bronze mirrors]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Archaeology of Japan]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Https://shinto.miraheze.org/w/index.php?search=]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:last 150 tagging]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ko:%EC%A0%95%EB%AC%B8%EA%B2%BD]] [[ja:%E5%A4%9A%E9%88%95%E7%B4%B0%E6%96%87%E9%8F%A1]][[ja:&title=Special%3ASearch&wprov=acrw1 -1]]

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - **Yoshitake Takagi Site** ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - **Ōgōri Wakayama Site** ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - **Uki Kumida Site** ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Masuda Site** ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - **Satodahara Site** ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - **Kajikuri Beach Site** ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Nagura Site** ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ōgōri Site** ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - **Bancheon-ri Site** ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ipsil-ri Site** ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - **Yoshitake Takagi Site** ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - **Ōgōri Wakayama Site** ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - **Uki Kumida Site** ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Masuda Site** ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - **Satodahara Site** ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - **Kajikuri Beach Site** ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Nagura Site** ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ōgōri Site** ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - **Bancheon-ri Site** ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ipsil-ri Site** ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

{{Kofun navbar}}[[Category:kk]] [[ko:%EC%A0%95%EB%AC%B8%EA%B2%BD]] [[ja:%E5%A4%9A%E9%88%95%E7%B4%B0%E6%96%87%E9%8F%A1]][[ja:&title=Special%3ASearch&wprov=acrw1 -1]]

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - **Yoshitake Takagi Site** ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - **Ōgōri Wakayama Site** ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - **Uki Kumida Site** ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Masuda Site** ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - **Satodahara Site** ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - **Kajikuri Beach Site** ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - **Nagura Site** ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ōgōri Site** ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - **Bancheon-ri Site** ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - **Ipsil-ri Site** ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

{{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[ko:%EC%A0%95%EB%AC%B8%EA%B2%BD]] [[ja:%E5%A4%9A%E9%88%95%E7%B4%B0%E6%96%87%E9%8F%A1]][[ja:&title=Special%3ASearch&wprov=acrw1 -1]]

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:File]][[Category:File]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ko:%EC%A0%95%EB%AC%B8%EA%B2%BD]] [[ja:%E5%A4%9A%E9%88%95%E7%B4%B0%E6%96%87%E9%8F%A1]][[ja:&title=Special%3ASearch&wprov=acrw1 -1]]

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:File]][[Category:File]] [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[ko:%EC%A0%95%EB%AC%B8%EA%B2%BD]] [[ja:%E5%A4%9A%E9%88%95%E7%B4%B0%E6%96%87%E9%8F%A1]][[ja:&title=Special%3ASearch&wprov=acrw1 -1]]

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:File]][[Category:File]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Multibossed mirrors]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ko:%EC%A0%95%EB%AC%B8%EA%B2%BD]] [[ja:%E5%A4%9A%E9%88%95%E7%B4%B0%E6%96%87%E9%8F%A1]][[ja:&title=Special%3ASearch&wprov=acrw1 -1]]

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡}}''' (たちゅうさいもんきょう<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Chinese mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: - '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> - '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> - '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> - '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> - '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市}})<ref name="sadamori"/> - '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: - '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> - '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}
