[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|12=draft}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社|12=draft}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|12=draft}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|12=draft}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|12=draft}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省|12=draft}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市|12=draft}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞|12=draft}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|12=draft}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|12=draft}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|12=draft}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|12=draft}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|12=draft}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|12=draft}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|12=draft}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|12=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|12=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|12=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|12=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|12=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}

Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|12=draft}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|12=draft}}.

== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|12=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|12=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|12=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|12=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|12=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|12=draft}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社|12=draft}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|12=draft}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|12=draft}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|12=draft}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省|12=draft}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市|12=draft}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞|12=draft}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|12=draft}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|12=draft}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|12=draft}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|12=draft}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|12=draft}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|12=draft}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|12=draft}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|12=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|12=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|12=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|12=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|12=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}

revision.text:
[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|12=draft}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社|12=draft}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|12=draft}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|12=draft}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|12=draft}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省|12=draft}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市|12=draft}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞|12=draft}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|12=draft}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|12=draft}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|12=draft}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|12=draft}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|12=draft}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|12=draft}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|12=draft}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|12=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|12=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|12=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|12=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|12=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}
{{citation needed|Tachū Saimonkyō}} <!--Multibossed mirrors--> <!--Multibossed mirrors--> <!--Multibossed mirrors--> <!--Multibossed mirrors--> <!--Multibossed mirrors--> <!--Multibossed mirrors--> <!--Multibossed mirrors-->

== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|12=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|12=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|12=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|12=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|12=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|12=draft}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社|12=draft}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|12=draft}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|12=draft}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|12=draft}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省|12=draft}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市|12=draft}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞|12=draft}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|12=draft}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|12=draft}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|12=draft}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|12=draft}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|12=draft}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|12=draft}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|12=draft}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|12=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|12=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|12=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|12=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|12=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|12=draft}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|12=draft}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|12=draft}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社|12=draft}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|12=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|12=draft}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|12=draft}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|12=draft}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|12=draft}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|12=draft}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省|12=draft}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市|12=draft}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞|12=draft}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|12=draft}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|12=draft}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|12=draft}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|12=draft}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|12=draft}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|12=draft}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|12=draft}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|12=draft}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|12=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|12=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|12=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|12=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|12=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}

== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|12=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|12=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|12=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|12=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|12=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|12=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}

20 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:多鈕細文鏡 up to rev 102207208
revision.text:
[[ファイル:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|大阪府柏原市大県出土
多鈕細文鏡}}{{small|[[東京国立博物館]]展示。}}]] '''多鈕細文鏡'''（たちゅうさいもんきょう[[小林行雄]]「たちゅう-さいもんきょう 多鈕細文鏡」 [[水野清一]]・小林行雄・編『図解考古学辞典』 [[東京創元社]] 1959年 P.620-621定森英夫「多鈕細文鏡」 [[江坂輝弥]]・[[芹沢長介]]・[[坂詰秀一]]・編『日本考古学小辞典』 ニュー・サイエンス社 1983年 P.205）は、紀元前6世紀頃に中国の[[遼寧省]]付近で発祥{{Cite web|和書 |title=国指定重要文化財 多鈕細文鏡 |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=小郡市埋蔵文化財調査センター（古代体験館おごおり） |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}した[[銅鏡]]で、紀元前2世紀前後に、対馬海峡の島々や九州北部、朝鮮半島南端にもほぼ同時に伝わったとされ、日本に初めて伝わった[[銅鏡]]である。 == 概要 == 名称は、'''多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡'''を略したものである。鏡の裏面に紐を通す鈕（ちゅう）が2、3個あり（多鈕）、細い線で鋸歯文などの[[幾何学]]紋様を施した（細文）銅鏡{{Cite web|和書|title=42青銅の匠の技|url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=佐賀市 |year= 2010|accessdate=2022-09-11}}で、[[中国鏡]]と異なり鏡面が[[凹面鏡]]となり{{Kotobank||2=日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）、ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典}}、縁の断面は[[蒲鉾]]状になる。直径は大半が9-12cmだが、大県遺跡の出土品は直径22cmである。 [[日本列島]]では製造されなかったとされるが、2015年に須玖タカウタ遺跡（福岡県[[春日市]]）から[[鋳型]]の破片が出土し、日本列島でも製造された可能性が指摘されている{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=国内最古の青銅鏡鋳型か　福岡の遺跡、製作史覆す可能性|author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=『[[日本経済新聞]]』|accessdate=2022-9-11}}{{Cite web|和書|title=須玖タカウタ遺跡2・5次調査出土青銅器生産関連遺物および土器類|url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=文化財課調査保存担当 |work=春日市 |year= 2022|accessdate=2022-09-11}}{{Cite web|和書|title=里田原遺跡出土遺物｜HIRADOじかん情報｜長崎県平戸市（ひらどし）ホームページ|url=https://www.city.hirado.nagasaki.jp/kurashi/culture/bunka/hiradoisan/hi23.html |author=平戸市文化観光商工部文化交流課文化遺産班 |work=平戸市 |year= 2016|accessdate=2022-09-11}}。 日本列島に最初に伝わったのは、[[弥生時代]]中期前半に対馬海峡の島々や九州北部である。それ以降、長い時間をかけて、本州、さらに[[中部地方]]（[[長野県]]）までの大変広い範囲にまで流布した。 == 用途 == 化粧道具ではないかという見解や、中国の古典でいう太陽の光を集めて火をとる採火器陽燧（ようすい）が多鈕細文鏡ではないかとの見解も出ている。一種の呪術具で、鏡は一つの性格として太陽信仰に結びつくものともされる。 日本列島では、[[九州]]北部・[[本州]]西端と本州中部で出土状況が異なる。九州北部と本州西端では、威信財として[[銅剣]]や[[銅矛]]、[[銅戈]]などの青銅器と共に埋納されていた。このことは、地域の支配者達の副葬品に定型化が急速に出来つつあったと考えられる。例外的に、[[福岡県]][[小郡市]]の小郡若山遺跡では、穴の中に2枚合わせで埋められ、その上に穴をあけた土器が被せられた状態で出土した。本州中部では、墓からではなく祭祀遺跡から出土する。長野県佐久市野沢地区原遺跡では、破片を加工し二つの孔をあけて呪符か護符に転用された形で出土した。 == 主な出土地 == 朝鮮半島を中心に、一部は[[遼寧省]]や[[沿海州]]、さらに日本列島など東北アジアの一角に分布する。 === 日本 === 日本列島では12例の出土例があり、うち8例が九州北部および山口県で出土した。 福岡県[[福岡市]][[早良区]]の[[吉武高木遺跡]]3号木棺墓からは、多鈕細文鏡が2本の銅剣や銅矛、銅戈、さらにヒスイ製の勾玉や95個の碧玉製管玉と共に出土した。剣・鏡・玉という[[三種の神器]]を彷彿とさせる器物が出土した3号木棺墓は「最古の王墓」と呼ばれ{{Cite web|和書|title=【重要遺構1特定集団墓】１．最古の王墓発見か？｜遺跡のみどころ｜国史跡吉武高木遺跡「やよいの風公園」福岡市の文化財|url=https://bunkazai.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/yoshitaketakagi/know/index.html |author= |work=福岡市経済観光文化局文化財活用部文化財活用課 |year= 2017|accessdate=2022-09-11}}、この遺跡が[[魏志倭人伝]]の[[伊都国]]と[[奴国]]との中間地点にあたり、その郡名をとった「早良国」という名で話題となった。この多鈕細文鏡は、他の副葬品と共に国の重要文化財に指定された。 このほか、小郡若山遺跡（福岡県小郡市、国指定重要文化財、埋納遺構）や、[[宇木汲田遺跡]]（[[佐賀県]][[唐津市]]、国指定重要文化財）、増田遺跡（佐賀県[[佐賀市]]、県指定重要文化財）{{Cite web|和書|title=増田遺跡甕棺墓出土多鈕細文鏡　一面（附）　甕棺　一基（二箇） - さがの歴史・文化お宝帳|url=https://www.saga-otakara.jp/search/detail.html?cultureId=5287&cityId=8 |author= |work=佐賀市地域振興部文化財課 |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}、[[里田原遺跡]]（[[長崎県]][[平戸市]]、県指定有形文化財）{{Cite web|和書|title=長崎県の文化財|url=https://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/360 |author= |work=長崎県教育庁学芸文化課 |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}、[[梶栗浜遺跡]]（山口県[[下関市]]）{{Cite web|和書|title=史跡の道～悠久の時間旅行に出かけよう～｜展示|url=https://www.shimo-kouko.jp/exhibition/shisekinomichi/ |author= |work=[[下関市立考古博物館]] |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}、名柄遺跡（[[奈良県]][[御所市]]、国指定重要文化財）でも副葬品として出土した。大県遺跡（[[大阪府]][[柏原市]]）の出土品は、開墾中に出土し持主を転々とした{{Cite web|和書|title=多鈕細文鏡（たちゅうさいもんきょう）―その数奇な運命―｜大阪府柏原市|url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=大阪府柏原市文化財課 |work=大阪府柏原市文化財課 |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}が、単独で出土したとされる。 === 朝鮮半島 === 反川里遺跡（[[朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|北朝鮮]][[平安南道]][[大同郡]]）[[梅原末治]]「[https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/ase1911/45/8/45_8_301/_pdf/-char/ja 朝鮮に於ける新發見の銅劍銅鉾並に關係の遺物]」 [[日本人類学会|東京人類学会]]『人類学雑誌』第45巻第7号 1930年 P.302-304や入室里遺跡（[[大韓民国]][[慶尚北道]][[慶州市]]）{{Kotobank||2=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典}}で副葬品として出土した例が知られる。2015年にも虎岩洞遺跡（大韓民国[[忠清北道]][[忠州市]]）の木棺墓から、7本の銅剣や3本の銅矛などと共に出土した{{cite news|url=http://japan.hani.co.kr/arti/culture/34343.html |title=高句麗碑が立つ中原に国立博物館建設なるか？|author=オ・ユンジュ |date=2019-9-12|publisher=『[[ハンギョレ]]』日本語版|accessdate=2022-9-11}}。 == 中国鏡との比較 == 大きさは、両者ともほぼ同じで、形もともに円形である。 多鈕細文鏡と中国鏡との間には多少の違いが見られる。鈕の数は前者が2、3個に対して後者が一つ、鏡面は前者が凹面で後者が平面かやや凸面、文様は前者が幾何学文で後者が[[神仙|神仙界]]の図文ほか多様、銘文は前者にはないが後者にはある場合が多い。多鈕細文鏡が流行した時期より50～100年後、中国鏡が大量にもたらされた弥生時代中期後半になると、地域の支配者の墓に、中国や朝鮮半島には例のないほどの大量埋納が行われた。これは倭人社会特有の現象であり、この習俗は[[古墳時代]]にまで継続されることになる。 == ギャラリー == ファイル:伝韓国慶尚南道出土 多鈕細文鏡 (TJ-4943).JPG|伝韓国慶尚南道出土鏡（重要美術品）
{{small|東京国立博物館展示。}} ファイル:奈良県御所市名柄字田中出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|御所市名柄出土鏡（重要文化財）
{{small|奈良県御所市。東京国立博物館展示。}} ファイル:吉武高木遺跡出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|[[吉武高木遺跡]]出土鏡（重要文化財）
{{small|福岡県福岡市。大阪歴史博物館企画展示時に撮影。}} == 出典 == == 関連項目 == * [[弥生時代]] {{デフォルトソート:たちゆうさいもんきよう}} [[Category:銅鏡]] [[Category:考古学]]

|work=平戸市 |year= 2016|accessdate=2022-09-11}}。 日本列島に最初に伝わったのは、[[弥生時代]]中期前半に対馬海峡の島々や九州北部である。それ以降、長い時間をかけて、本州、さらに[[中部地方]]（[[長野県]]）までの大変広い範囲にまで流布した。 == 用途 == == 主な出土地 == 朝鮮半島を中心に、一部は[[遼寧省]]や[[沿海州]]、さらに日本列島など東北アジアの一角に分布する。 === 日本 === 日本列島では12例の出土例があり、うち8例が九州北部および山口県で出土した。 福岡県[[福岡市]][[早良区]]の[[吉武高木遺跡]]3号木棺墓からは、多鈕細文鏡が2本の銅剣や銅矛、銅戈、さらにヒスイ製の勾玉や95個の碧玉製管玉と共に出土した。剣・鏡・玉という[[三種の神器]]を彷彿とさせる器物が出土した3号木棺墓は「最古の王墓」と呼ばれ{{Cite web|和書|title=【重要遺構1特定集団墓】１．最古の王墓発見か？｜遺跡のみどころ｜国史跡吉武高木遺跡「やよいの風公園」福岡市の文化財|url=https://bunkazai.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/yoshitaketakagi/know/index.html |author= |work=福岡市経済観光文化局文化財活用部文化財活用課 |year= 2017|accessdate=2022-09-11}}、この遺跡が[[魏志倭人伝]]の[[伊都国]]と[[奴国]]との中間地点にあたり、その郡名をとった「早良国」という名で話題となった。この多鈕細文鏡は、他の副葬品と共に国の重要文化財に指定された。 このほか、小郡若山遺跡（福岡県小郡市、国指定重要文化財、埋納遺構）や、[[宇木汲田遺跡]]（[[佐賀県]][[唐津市]]、国指定重要文化財）、増田遺跡（佐賀県[[佐賀市]]、県指定重要文化財）{{Cite web|和書|title=増田遺跡甕棺墓出土多鈕細文鏡　一面（附）　甕棺　一基（二箇） - さがの歴史・文化お宝帳|url=https://www.saga-otakara.jp/search/detail.html?cultureId=5287&cityId=8 |author= |work=佐賀市地域振興部文化財課 |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}、[[里田原遺跡]]（[[長崎県]][[平戸市]]、県指定有形文化財）{{Cite web|和書|title=長崎県の文化財|url=https://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/360 |author= |work=長崎県教育庁学芸文化課 |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}、[[梶栗浜遺跡]]（山口県[[下関市]]）{{Cite web|和書|title=史跡の道～悠久の時間旅行に出かけよう～｜展示|url=https://www.shimo-kouko.jp/exhibition/shisekinomichi/ |author= |work=[[下関市立考古博物館]] |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}、名柄遺跡（[[奈良県]][[御所市]]、国指定重要文化財）でも副葬品として出土した。大県遺跡（[[大阪府]][[柏原市]]）の出土品は、開墾中に出土し持主を転々とした{{Cite web|和書|title=多鈕細文鏡（たちゅうさいもんきょう）―その数奇な運命―｜大阪府柏原市|url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=大阪府柏原市文化財課 |work=大阪府柏原市文化財課 |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}が、単独で出土したとされる。 === 朝鮮半島 === 反川里遺跡（[[朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|北朝鮮]][[平安南道]][[大同郡]]）[[梅原末治]]「[https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/ase1911/45/8/45_8_301/_pdf/-char/ja 朝鮮に於ける新發見の銅劍銅鉾並に關係の遺物]」 [[日本人類学会|東京人類学会]]『人類学雑誌』第45巻第7号 1930年 P.302-304や入室里遺跡（[[大韓民国]][[慶尚北道]][[慶州市]]）{{Kotobank||2=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典}}で副葬品として出土した例が知られる。2015年にも虎岩洞遺跡（大韓民国[[忠清北道]][[忠州市]]）の木棺墓から、7本の銅剣や3本の銅矛などと共に出土した{{cite news|url=http://japan.hani.co.kr/arti/culture/34343.html |title=高句麗碑が立つ中原に国立博物館建設なるか？|author=オ・ユンジュ |date=2019-9-12|publisher=『[[ハンギョレ]]』日本語版|accessdate=2022-9-11}}。 == 中国鏡との比較 == 大きさは、両者ともほぼ同じで、形もともに円形である。 多鈕細文鏡と中国鏡との間には多少の違いが見られる。鈕の数は前者が2、3個に対して後者が一つ、鏡面は前者が凹面で後者が平面かやや凸面、文様は前者が幾何学文で後者が[[神仙|神仙界]]の図文ほか多様、銘文は前者にはないが後者にはある場合が多い。多鈕細文鏡が流行した時期より50～100年後、中国鏡が大量にもたらされた弥生時代中期後半になると、地域の支配者の墓に、中国や朝鮮半島には例のないほどの大量埋納が行われた。これは倭人社会特有の現象であり、この習俗は[[古墳時代]]にまで継続されることになる。 == ギャラリー == ファイル:伝韓国慶尚南道出土 多鈕細文鏡 (TJ-4943).JPG|伝韓国慶尚南道出土鏡（重要美術品）
{{small|東京国立博物館展示。}} ファイル:奈良県御所市名柄字田中出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|御所市名柄出土鏡（重要文化財）
{{small|奈良県御所市。東京国立博物館展示。}} ファイル:吉武高木遺跡出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|[[吉武高木遺跡]]出土鏡（重要文化財）
{{small|福岡県福岡市。大阪歴史博物館企画展示時に撮影。}} == 出典 == == 関連項目 == * [[弥生時代]] {{デフォルトソート:たちゆうさいもんきよう}} [[Category:銅鏡]] [[Category:考古学]]

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社|qq=draft}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省|qq=draft}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|qq=draft}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市|qq=draft}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞|qq=draft}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|qq=draft}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|qq=draft}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|qq=draft}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|qq=draft}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|qq=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|qq=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|qq=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|qq=draft}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|qq=draft}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|qq=draft}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|qq=draft}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|qq=draft}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft}}

Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo National Museum|qq=}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Sogensha|qq=}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze mirror|qq=}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liaoning Province|qq=}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima Strait|qq=}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga, Fukuoka|qq=}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Keizai Shimbun|qq=}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima Strait|qq=}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saku, Nagano|qq=}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|qq=}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Regalia of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Regalia of Japan|qq=}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgōri, Fukuoka|qq=}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karatsu, Saga|qq=}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga City|qq=}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirado, Nagasaki|qq=}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|qq=}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gose, Nara|qq=}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwara, Osaka|qq=}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeongju, South Korea|qq=}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Multibossed mirrors]]
revision.text:
[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo National Museum|qq=}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|Tokyo Sogensha|ja|東京創元社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Sogensha|qq=}}, 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze mirror|qq=}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning Province|ja|遼寧省|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liaoning Province|qq=}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima Strait|qq=}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|ja|春日市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga, Fukuoka|qq=}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|Nihon Keizai Shimbun|ja|日本経済新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Keizai Shimbun|qq=}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|ja|対馬海峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima Strait|qq=}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagano Prefecture|qq=}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saku, Nagano|qq=}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|ja|早良区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|qq=}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|ja|三種の神器|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Regalia of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Regalia of Japan|qq=}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ōgōri, Fukuoka|ja|小郡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgōri, Fukuoka|qq=}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|ja|唐津市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karatsu, Saga|qq=}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga City|ja|佐賀市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saga City|qq=}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|ja|平戸市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirado, Nagasaki|qq=}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|ja|下関市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|qq=}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gose, Nara|qq=}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|ja|柏原市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiwara, Osaka|qq=}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju, South Korea|ja|慶州市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeongju, South Korea|qq=}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}}

Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ([[:en:Tokyo Sogensha|Tokyo Sogensha]], 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] that originated around the 6th |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>

Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=}}.

=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
