[[sn:Multibossed mirrors]] [[en:Multibossed mirrors]] [[ko:%EC%A0%95%EB%AC%B8%EA%B2%BD]] [[ja:%E5%A4%9A%E9%88%95%E7%B4%B0%E6%96%87%E9%8F%A1]] [[ja:&title=Special%3ASearch&wprov=acrw1 -1]]
<!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors--><!--Multibossed mirrors-->

[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ([[:en:Tokyo Sogensha|Tokyo Sogensha]], 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] that originated around the 6th century BCE near [[:en:Liaoning|Liaoning Province]], China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi == Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ([[:en:Tokyo Sogensha|Tokyo Sogensha]], 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北隆館}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] that originated around the 6th century BCE near [[:en:Liaoning|Liaoning Province]], China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Kofun template and pages that transclude it
revision.text:
[[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ([[:en:Tokyo Sogensha|Tokyo Sogensha]], 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北隆館}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] that originated around the 6th century BCE near [[:en:Liaoning|Liaoning Province]], China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ([[:en:Tokyo Sogensha|Tokyo Sogensha]], 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北隆館}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] that originated around the 6th century BCE near [[:en:Liaoning|Liaoning Province]], China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.

They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神}}.

多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ([[:en:Tokyo Sogensha|Tokyo Sogensha]], 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北隆館}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] that originated around the 6th century BCE near [[:en:Liaoning|Liaoning Province]], China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science that originated around the 6th century BCE near [[:en:Liaoning|Liaoning Province]], China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.

They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.

=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ([[:en:Tokyo Sogensha|Tokyo Sogensha]], 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北隆館}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] that originated around the 6th century BCE near [[:en:Liaoning|Liaoning Province]], China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q131456269]] Wikidata name: multi-knobbed fine-patterned mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:大阪府柏原市大県出土 多鈕細文鏡.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}} excavated in Ōgōri, Kashiwara, Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Saimonkyō|ja|多鈕細文鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Multibossed mirrors|qq=}}''' (多鈕細文鏡<ref name="kobayashi">{{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Mizuno Seiichi|ja|水野清一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuno Seiichi|qq=|1=Seiichi Mizuno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549425}} & Kobayashi Yukio (eds.), *Illustrated Archaeology Dictionary* ([[:en:Tokyo Sogensha|Tokyo Sogensha]], 1959), pp. 620–621.</ref><ref name="sadamori">{{ill|Sadamori Hideo|ja|定森英夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|qq=|1=Teruya Esaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550799}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|qq=|1=Serizawa Chōsuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616035}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一|1=Hideichi Sakazume|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425571}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北隆館|1=Hokuryukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404499}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] that originated around the 6th century BCE near [[:en:Liaoning|Liaoning Province]], China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]], northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher=[[:en:The Nikkei|Nihon Keizai Shimbun]] |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the [[:en:Korea Strait|Tsushima Strait]] and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神|1=solar deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q511056}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|qq=|1=dōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3045644}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|qq=|1=dōhoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11650189}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|qq=|1=dōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11650186}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]

== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ([[:en:Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka]], Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]].<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ([[:en:Ogōri, Fukuoka|Ōgōri, Fukuoka]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ([[:en:Karatsu, Saga|Karatsu, Saga]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ([[:en:Saga (city)|Saga City]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ([[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ([[:en:Shimonoseki|Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi]])<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ([[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ([[:en:Kashiwara|Kashiwara, Osaka]]): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ([[:en:Gyeongju|Gyeongju, South Korea]], South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> == See Also == * [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]]
