wikidata|qid=Q11425571}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北隆館|1=Hokuryukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11404499}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning|en|Liaoning|qid=Q43934|lt=Liaoning Province|1=Liaoning}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Korea Strait|en|Korea Strait|qid=Q52052|lt=Tsushima Strait|1=Korea Strait}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.
== Overview == The name is an abbreviation of "Tachū Saisenkyohamonkyō" (多鈕細線鋸歯文鏡).<ref name="kobayashi"/> These mirrors feature two or three loops (鈕, *chū*) for threading cords<ref name="sadamori"/> and are decorated with geometric patterns, including fine linear sawtooth designs (鋸歯文, *kyohamon*).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Unlike Han-style mirrors, they have a concave reflective surface<ref name="Kotobank">{{Kotobank||2=Japan Encyclopedia (Nipponica), Britannica International Encyclopedia}}</ref> and a cross-section shaped like a semi-cylinder (蒲鉾状).<ref name="kobayashi"/><ref name="sadamori"/> Most have a diameter of 9–12 cm, but one found at the Ōgōri site measures 22 cm.<ref name="kobayashi"/>
They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|en|Kasuga, Fukuoka|qid=Q47084|lt=Kasuga, Fukuoka|1=Kasuga, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|The Nikkei|en|The Nikkei|qid=Q1077694|lt=Nihon Keizai Shimbun|1=The Nikkei}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Korea Strait|en|Korea Strait|qid=Q52052|lt=Tsushima Strait|1=Korea Strait}} and northern Kyushu. Over time, they were distributed widely across Honshu, reaching as far as {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}} in central Japan.
== Purpose == It is debated whether these mirrors were used as cosmetic tools or as ritual objects. Some theories suggest they functioned as {{ill|fire-starting mirrors|ja|陽燧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fire-starting mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which focused sunlight to ignite flames.<ref name="Kotobank"/> They are often considered shamanic items, as mirrors are commonly linked to {{ill|solar worship|ja|太陽信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar worship|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽神|1=Solar deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q511056}}.
In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|1=dōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3045644}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|1=dōhoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11650189}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|1=dōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11650186}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|en|Saku, Nagano|qid=Q495821|lt=Saku, Nagano|1=Saku, Nagano}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.
== Key Excavation Sites == They have been unearthed across northeast Asia, particularly in Korea, Liaoning, and the Russian Primorsky region.<ref name="sadamori"/>
=== Japan === There are 12 recorded finds in Japan,<ref name="ogori"/><ref name="hirado"/> with eight from northern Kyushu and Yamaguchi Prefecture.<ref name="ogori"/>
Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|en|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|qid=Q1071779|lt=Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|1=Sawara-ku, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ogōri, Fukuoka|en|Ogōri, Fukuoka|qid=Q683248|lt=Ōgōri, Fukuoka|1=Ogōri, Fukuoka}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|en|Karatsu, Saga|qid=Q738275|lt=Karatsu, Saga|1=Karatsu, Saga}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga (city)|en|Saga (city)|qid=Q209766|lt=Saga City|1=Saga (city)}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|en|Hirado, Nagasaki|qid=Q328601|lt=Hirado, Nagasaki|1=Hirado, Nagasaki}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|qid=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|1=Shimonoseki}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|qid=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara|1=Gose, Nara}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara|en|Kashiwara|qid=Q873583|lt=Kashiwara, Osaka|1=Kashiwara}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18808}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju|en|Gyeongju|qid=Q42097|lt=Gyeongju, South Korea|1=Gyeongju}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
== See Also == * {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}}

Osaka}}{{small|Exhibited at {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}.}}]] '''{{ill|Multibossed mirrors|lt=Tachū Hideo|ja|定森英夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadamori Hideo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Tachū Saimonkyō," in {{ill|Esaka Teruya|ja|江坂輝弥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esaka Teruya|1=Teruya Esaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11550799}}, {{ill|Serizawa Chōsuke|ja|芹沢長介|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serizawa Chōsuke|1=Serizawa Chōsuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11616035}}, & {{ill|Sakazume Shūichi|ja|坂詰秀一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakazume Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 坂誥秀一|1=Hideichi Sakazume|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11425571}} (eds.), *Japanese Archaeology Dictionary* ({{ill|New Science Publishing|ja|ニュー・サイエンス社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Science Publishing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北隆館|1=Hokuryukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11404499}}, 1983), p. 205.</ref>) is a type of {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} that originated around the 6th century BCE near {{ill|Liaoning|en|Liaoning|qid=Q43934|lt=Liaoning Province|1=Liaoning}}, China.<ref name="Kotobank" /><ref name="ogori">{{Cite web|和書 |title=Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property: Tachū Saimonkyō |url=https://www.kodaitaiken-ogori.jp/pdf/1591942192.pdf |author= |work=Ōgōri City Archaeological Research Center (Kodai Taikenkan Ogori) |year=2020 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> It is believed to have spread almost simultaneously in the 2nd century BCE across the islands of the {{ill|Korea Strait|en|Korea Strait|qid=Q52052|lt=Tsushima Strait|1=Korea Strait}}, northern Kyushu, and the southern Korean Peninsula, making it the first bronze mirror introduced to Japan.

They were not originally thought to have been produced in Japan,<ref name="saga">{{Cite web|和書|title=42: The Art of Bronze in Saga |url=https://www.city.saga.lg.jp/site_files/file/usefiles/downloads/s26052_20101216043213.pdf |author= |work=Saga City |year=2010 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref> but in 2015, a fragment of a mold was excavated at the {{ill|Sugu Takauta Site|ja|須玖タカウタ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugu Takauta Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|en|Kasuga, Fukuoka|qid=Q47084|lt=Kasuga, Fukuoka|1=Kasuga, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture), suggesting they may have been manufactured domestically.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG27H9E_X20C15A5CR8000/ |title=Japan’s Oldest Bronze Mirror Mold? Discovery at a Fukuoka Archaeological Site May Rewrite History |author= |date=2015-5-27|publisher={{ill|The Nikkei|en|The Nikkei|qid=Q1077694|lt=Nihon Keizai Shimbun|1=The Nikkei}} |accessdate=2022-9-11}}</ref><ref name="kasuga">{{Cite web|和書|title=Sugu Takauta Excavation: Bronze Production Evidence |url=https://www.city.kasuga.fukuoka.jp/miryoku/history/historymuseum/1002286/1002288/1006247.html |author=Kasuga City Cultural Property Division |work=Kasuga City |year=2022 |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
The mirrors first reached the Japanese archipelago in the early {{ill|Middle Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代中期前半|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Yayoi period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, spreading from the islands of the {{ill|Korea Strait|en|Korea Strait|qid=Q52052|lt=Tsushima Strait|1=Korea Strait}} and northern Kyushu. In Japan, their burial patterns vary: - In northern Kyushu and western Honshu, they were interred with prestige items such as {{ill|bronze swords|ja|銅剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze swords|1=dōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3045644}}, {{ill|bronze halberds|ja|銅矛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze halberds|1=dōhoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11650189}}, and {{ill|bronze dagger-axes|ja|銅戈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze dagger-axes|1=dōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11650186}}, suggesting they were symbols of power.<ref name="Kotobank"/> - In contrast, in central Honshu, they were found in ritual sites rather than graves.<ref name="Kotobank"/> At the {{ill|Hara Site|ja|原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Saku, Nagano|en|Saku, Nagano|qid=Q495821|lt=Saku, Nagano|1=Saku, Nagano}}, a broken mirror fragment with two holes was repurposed as an amulet.

Notable sites include: * '''Yoshitake Takagi Site''' ({{ill|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|en|Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|qid=Q1071779|lt=Sawara-ku, Fukuoka|1=Sawara-ku, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka): Found in Tomb 3 with two bronze swords, a bronze halberd, a bronze dagger-axe, and jade beads, resembling the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}.<ref name="yayoinokaze"/> * '''Ōgōri Wakayama Site''' ({{ill|Ogōri, Fukuoka|en|Ogōri, Fukuoka|qid=Q683248|lt=Ōgōri, Fukuoka|1=Ogōri, Fukuoka}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="ogori"/> * '''Uki Kumida Site''' ({{ill|Karatsu, Saga|en|Karatsu, Saga|qid=Q738275|lt=Karatsu, Saga|1=Karatsu, Saga}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Masuda Site''' ({{ill|Saga (city)|en|Saga (city)|qid=Q209766|lt=Saga City|1=Saga (city)}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="saga"/> * '''Satodahara Site''' ({{ill|Hirado, Nagasaki|en|Hirado, Nagasaki|qid=Q328601|lt=Hirado, Nagasaki|1=Hirado, Nagasaki}}, Prefectural Cultural Property)<ref name="hirado"/> * '''Kajikuri Beach Site''' ({{ill|Shimonoseki|en|Shimonoseki|qid=Q203308|lt=Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi|1=Shimonoseki}})<ref name="sadamori"/> * '''Nagura Site''' ({{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|qid=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara|1=Gose, Nara}}, Nationally Designated Important Cultural Property)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ōgōri Site''' ({{ill|Kashiwara|en|Kashiwara|qid=Q873583|lt=Kashiwara, Osaka|1=Kashiwara}}): Discovered during land development.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=Tachū Saimonkyō: A Mysterious Journey |url=http://www.city.kashiwara.osaka.jp/docs/2014082200249/?doc_id=1515|author=Kashiwara City Cultural Property Division |work=Kashiwara City |year= |accessdate=2022-09-11}}</ref>
=== Korea === Examples have been excavated from: * '''Bancheon-ri Site''' ({{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18808}}, North Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/> * '''Ipsil-ri Site''' ({{ill|Gyeongju|en|Gyeongju|qid=Q42097|lt=Gyeongju, South Korea|1=Gyeongju}}, South Korea)<ref name="kobayashi"/>
